channel.title: Opensource Sunday – Matthew 6
channel.description: Recent content in Matthew 6 on Opensource Sunday

item.title: Lesson 4 · When You Pray
item.description:
Scripture: Matthew 6:5–15 Series: Sermon on the Mount

Behind the Curtain

What do I need to know before teaching?

Matthew 6 opens with Jesus warning about “acts of righteousness” performed for show (v.1). He works through three of them — giving (vv.2–4), praying (vv.5–15), and fasting (vv.16–18) — and each time he says the same thing: don’t do this for the camera. Do it in the quiet, for an audience of One.

Tonight we camp on prayer. A few things worth holding onto:

  • Verses 5–6 — Don’t pray to be seen. Jesus isn’t against public prayer (he led it himself). He’s against prayer as a performance. Go into your room, close the door, pray to your Father who sees in secret.
  • Verses 7–8 — Don’t babble. Jesus names a specific failure: piling on words to manipulate God. He says: your Father already knows what you need before you ask. That’s actually a comfort, not a criticism. Prayer isn’t informing God. It’s connecting with him.
  • Verses 9–13 — The Lord’s Prayer. This is the model. Six short petitions. Nothing fancy. It takes 20 seconds to pray.
  • Verses 14–15 — Jesus returns to one line of the prayer and underlines it: forgive as you have been forgiven. The forgiveness we offer mirrors the forgiveness we’ve received. It’s not that forgiveness is earned — it’s that a forgiven heart can’t stay unforgiving.

The Lord’s Prayer, phrase by phrase:

  • “Our Father in heaven” — Prayer begins with relationship, not religion. “Father” (Abba, in Mark’s parallel) is how a child addresses a dad. “Our” — not just mine, but ours; the church prays together.
  • “Hallowed be your name” — May your name be treated as holy. A plea that God’s reputation wouldn’t be cheapened — starting with my own life.
  • “Your kingdom come, your will be done on earth as it is in heaven” — Not my will first. Yours. Heaven’s rhythms breaking into earth.
  • “Give us today our daily bread” — Real, practical, today-sized needs. Not a retirement plan — a lunch.
  • “Forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors” — The hinge. We receive mercy in the same measure we extend it (which is why v.14–15 underlines this).
  • “Lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one” — Protection. Keep us out of places where we know we’ll crumble.

The big idea: Jesus is de-scaring prayer. Middle schoolers often think prayer is performance, or that they’ll say the wrong thing, or that they need fancy words. Jesus says: stop. You already have a Father who already knows. Talk to him like a kid.

A pastoral word on the word “Father.” For some students, “Father” is a loaded word. Their earthly father may be absent, angry, unsafe, or simply unknown. When Jesus teaches us to pray “Our Father in heaven,” he is not saying “God is exactly like your dad.” He’s saying “God is the Father you were always meant to have.” If you have a student in the room for whom “Father” lands heavy, don’t rush past it — name it out loud. Jesus is offering a Father who is perfectly caring, patient, forgiving, and present. That’s the Father every kid in the room deserves, and it’s the one they have in God.

Center Stage

What are the most important takeaways?

Prayer is a kid talking to a Father who already loves them. Short. Honest. Trusting. No performance required.

Prop Department

What supplies do I need?

  • Bibles, one per student
  • Whiteboard or large paper + markers
  • Optional: a microphone or large prop (used for the “performance prayer” demo)
  • Index cards — one per student
  • Pens/pencils
  • Christ light / candle + matches (for opening)
  • Optional: pre-printed cards of the Lord’s Prayer for students to take home

Directing the Scene

What learning activities might we do?

Start with Prayer

Light the Christ light. Pray a very short, very simple prayer out loud yourself — something like: “Jesus, thanks for this group. Help us to hear you tonight. Amen.” This models the whole lesson.

Catch Their Attention — The Big Show Prayer

Before the lesson: Ask another leader, or a student who can play along, to help. You’ll do two prayers back-to-back.

During the lesson:

Stand up, grab the microphone prop (or pretend). Say:

Okay, I’m going to pray. Get ready. This one’s gonna be powerful.

Then pray out loud, ridiculously over the top:

“O most high, magnificent, awesome, incredible God of the universe, who sits enthroned above the heavens, whose majesty is beyond words and whose glory knows no bounds, I come before you humbly, so humbly, so super humbly, to thank you and to praise you and to lift up this assembled gathering on this Wednesday evening…”

Keep going as long as the students will tolerate it.

Then without commentary, turn to the other leader/student. Say: “Your turn.”

They pray:

“God, thanks for this group. Help us hear you tonight. Amen.”

Look at the students:

Which one do you think Jesus likes better?

Let them answer. Then say:

Before we read tonight, I want to tell you this. Jesus has something very specific to say about how to pray — and it’s not what most people think.

Read and Interpret — Matthew 6:5–15

Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:

Jesus is in the middle of the Sermon on the Mount. He’s been talking about not doing good stuff for the camera. Now he zeroes in on one specific thing: prayer. He tells us two things NOT to do, and then — because Jesus is a good teacher — he shows us what to do instead.

Read Matthew 6:5–15 together. Go around the circle.

Discuss:

  1. In verses 5–6, what does Jesus say NOT to do when you pray? — Don’t pray to be seen. Don’t pray to impress. Go into your room, close the door.
  2. In verses 7–8, what’s the second thing Jesus says not to do? — Don’t babble. Don’t pile on words. Your Father already knows what you need.
  3. Does “your Father already knows what you need” make prayer more or less important? Why? — More. It means prayer isn’t us informing God. It’s us connecting with God. He already knows — he wants to hear from you anyway.
  4. Now read verses 9–13 again slowly. Notice anything? — It’s short. Almost weirdly short. Jesus is modeling what he just said.

Then walk the Lord’s Prayer phrase by phrase on the whiteboard. Write each phrase and stop to unpack it.

  • “Our Father in heaven” — Who are we talking to? Father. Not boss. Not cop. Not distant king. Dad. And “Our” — the whole family praying together.
    • Pause here if needed. Acknowledge the room: some of us have great dads, some of us don’t. Jesus isn’t saying God is like your dad. He’s saying God is the Father every one of you was made for — perfectly patient, perfectly present, perfectly kind. If “Father” is hard for you, stay with it. This is the one who doesn’t disappoint.
  • “Hallowed be your name” — “May your name be treated as holy.” Starting with my own life. Not dragging God’s name through the mud by how I live.
  • “Your kingdom come, your will be done on earth as it is in heaven” — Whose will comes first? Not mine. His. This is the line that makes prayer dangerous — because when you pray it and mean it, it changes what you want.
  • “Give us today our daily bread” — Real needs. Today’s needs. Not a five-year plan — a lunch, a ride, the algebra test.
  • “Forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors” — The big one. We ask God to forgive us in the same way we forgive others. Jesus comes back to this in verses 14–15 to underline it.
  • “Lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one” — Protection. Keep me out of the places where I know I fall apart.

Read verses 14–15 together again.

Ask:

  • Why does Jesus circle back and underline this one specific line? — Because a forgiven heart can’t stay unforgiving. If we’re walking around receiving God’s mercy but refusing to give any, something’s not wired right.

Say:

Here’s what Jesus just handed us. Prayer isn’t performance. You don’t score points with God by using big words. You don’t impress him with volume. You don’t manipulate him with repetition. You walk in as a kid, say what you mean, and trust your Father to hear you — because he already knows and he already loves you.

And when he teaches us the model, he keeps it short. Six petitions. Twenty seconds. But every phrase is doing work.

Ponder the Possibilities

Discuss together:

  1. What makes prayer feel awkward or hard for you — if it does?
  2. Jesus says “WHEN you pray,” not “if you pray.” Why do you think he assumes his followers are praying?
  3. Which phrase of the Lord’s Prayer hits different now that we’ve slowed it down? Why?
  4. Be honest: is there someone you’re asking God to forgive you, but you haven’t forgiven? What do you think Jesus would say to you about that?
  5. What’s one thing you don’t pray about right now — that you probably could?

Turn Toward Action — Pray It for a Week

Hand each student an index card (or a pre-printed Lord’s Prayer card if you have them). Say:

Here’s your challenge. Pray the Lord’s Prayer every day this week. In bed. At breakfast. On the bus. In class before a test. Doesn’t matter where. Just pray it.

Don’t rush it. Don’t mumble through it. Actually mean each phrase. See what happens after a week.

Pray the Lord’s Prayer together as a whole group to close the lesson:

Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name, your kingdom come, your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven. Give us today our daily bread. And forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors. And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one. Amen.

item.title: Lesson 5 · Treasures and Two Masters
item.description:
Scripture: Matthew 6:19–24 Series: Sermon on the Mount

Behind the Curtain

What do I need to know before teaching?

Jesus has just finished talking about prayer (last week). Before he gets to do not worry (next week), he drops six of the most quoted verses in the whole sermon — and ties them together with a single idea: what has your heart has your life.

  • Verses 19–21 — Two Treasures. “Do not store up for yourselves treasures on earth, where moths and vermin destroy, and where thieves break in and steal. But store up for yourselves treasures in heaven… For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.”
    • “Treasures on earth” — anything that can be eaten by moths, rusted by rain, or stolen by a thief. It decays. It runs out. It can be taken.
    • “Treasures in heaven” — things that last. Things God cares about. Love, faithfulness, mercy, the people you’ve helped, the forgiveness you’ve extended.
    • Verse 21 is the punchline. Where your treasure is, there your heart will be also. You follow your treasure like a magnet. Whatever you’re collecting, you’re becoming.
  • Verses 22–23 — The Lamp of the Eye. “The eye is the lamp of the body. If your eyes are healthy, your whole body will be full of light. But if your eyes are unhealthy, your whole body will be full of darkness.” Ancient metaphor: your eyes are the windows your body takes in light through. What you look at determines what fills you up.
  • Verse 24 — Two Masters. “No one can serve two masters… You cannot serve both God and money.” The word Jesus uses for money is mammon — it’s personified almost like a god. Jesus isn’t saying money is bad. He’s saying money makes a terrible god. You can use money. You can’t serve it.

The thread: Jesus is asking a penetrating question — what are you living for? What you store, what you look at, and who you serve all expose the same thing: your heart. And middle schoolers are already being shaped by what they’re collecting, watching, and worshipping. They just don’t always call it that.

Center Stage

What are the most important takeaways?

Whatever has your heart has your life. You cannot serve both God and money — pick one.

Prop Department

What supplies do I need?

  • Bibles, one per student
  • A physical “treasure pile” prop: a mix of stuff that decays/can-be-stolen (fake cash, a trophy, a phone, a jersey, a rusted old coin) and stuff that doesn’t (a Bible, a card with a person’s name, a cross, a photo of people serving)
  • Whiteboard or large paper + markers
  • Index cards — one per student
  • Pens/pencils
  • Christ light / candle + matches (for opening)

Directing the Scene

What learning activities might we do?

Start with Prayer

Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. Ask God to show each student where their treasure actually is tonight.

Catch Their Attention — The Burning House

Say:

Quick scenario. Your house is on fire. Everyone in your family is already out safe. The pets are out. You have exactly sixty seconds to run back in and grab ONE thing before the roof caves in. What do you grab?

Let every student answer. Write the answers on the whiteboard.

After the list is up, ask:

Look at this list. What does it say about us that these are the things we’d run back in for?

Say:

Whatever you would run back in for — that’s a treasure. Tonight Jesus has something to say about what kind of treasure will actually last.

Read and Interpret — Matthew 6:19–24

Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:

Jesus is in the middle of the Sermon on the Mount. He just taught us how to pray. Now, before he gets to that famous passage about not worrying, he drops six verses that hit HARD. Listen for a single idea that runs through all three parts.

Read Matthew 6:19–24 together. Go around the circle.

Discuss:

Treasures (vv.19–21):

  1. What are “treasures on earth”? Why does Jesus say not to store them up? — Stuff that rots, rusts, or gets stolen. It runs out. It can be taken.
  2. What are “treasures in heaven”? What lasts forever? — The people you helped. The mercy you showed. The forgiveness you gave. Your relationship with God. The ways you made God look good in the world.
  3. Verse 21 — “Where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.” What does that mean? — You follow your treasure. Whatever you’re collecting, you start to love. Whatever you love, you start to become.

The Lamp of the Eye (vv.22–23): 4. Jesus says the eye is the lamp of the body. What do you think he means? — What you look at fills you up. If you feed your eyes garbage, your insides go dark. If you feed your eyes on good stuff, you fill with light. 5. For a middle schooler, what are your eyes on most? (Be honest.) What’s filling you up right now?

Two Masters (v.24): 6. Jesus says you can’t serve TWO masters. Why not? — Because both want your whole heart. You end up loving one and hating the other. 7. Jesus specifically names ONE master that competes with God. What is it? — Money. And not just cash — “mammon,” the whole system of chasing stuff to feel secure or important. 8. Is Jesus saying money is evil? — No. He’s saying money makes a terrible god. You can use money. You can’t serve it.

Say:

Jesus is asking us one question three different ways tonight. What are you living for?

What you store up shows your heart. What you look at fills your heart. Who you serve owns your heart. And you can’t split your heart — the world says you can, Jesus says you can’t.

Here’s what nobody’s going to tell you at school: the stuff the world tells you to collect — the grades, the followers, the shoes, the spot on the team, the version of your body that’s in this year — all of it will rust, break, fade, or get forgotten. Every bit of it. Jesus isn’t saying those things are evil. He’s saying they make terrible treasures. They can’t hold your heart.

But the things God asks us to store up — love for the kid nobody sits with, forgiveness for the one who hurt us, time with him, faithfulness when no one sees — that stuff lasts forever.

Ponder the Possibilities

Discuss together:

  1. Based on how you spend your time, your money, and your attention — what are you actually treasuring right now?
  2. What’s one thing you look at a lot that you know is filling you with darkness, not light?
  3. Jesus says you can’t serve God AND money. Is there another “master” you’ve been low-key serving? (Popularity? Grades? A sport? A person?)
  4. If your heart really follows your treasure — what do you want your heart to become over the next year?
  5. Verse 21 says it bluntly: your heart goes where your treasure is. What does that mean you need to change about what you’re collecting?

Turn Toward Action — Move Some Treasure

Hand each student an index card. Say:

Three quick moves this week. Pick the one God is putting in front of you.

  1. Move something up to heaven. Spend time, money, or energy this week on someone who can’t pay you back. A sibling. A classmate. A kid nobody sits with.
  2. Fast your eyes from something. One app limit, one unfollow, one show you turn off. Something you know is filling you with darkness.
  3. Fire one of your masters. Name a thing that’s been running your life in secret — grades, a person’s opinion, followers. Out loud, tell God you’re not serving it anymore. Ask him to be your one master instead.

Write which one you picked and the first step.

Close with prayer. Read Matt 6:21 out loud one more time on your way out:

“For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.”

item.title: Lesson 6 · Do Not Worry
item.description:
Scripture: Matthew 6:25–34 Series: Sermon on the Mount

Behind the Curtain

What do I need to know before teaching?

Middle school is a worry factory. Grades. Friend drama. Body stuff. Family stuff. Tests. The lunch table. That group chat. The future. What people think. What God thinks. The pile never stops growing.

Jesus knows this. In Matthew 6:25–34, he walks right into the middle of it and says one of the most direct, pastoral things in the whole Sermon on the Mount: “Therefore I tell you, do not worry.”

A few things to know before you teach:

  • The “therefore” matters. This passage flows directly out of Matt 6:19–24, where Jesus says you cannot serve both God and money. So when he says “therefore do not worry,” he’s not handing out an inspirational quote. He’s saying: if your treasure is in heaven and your master is God, worry doesn’t fit anymore.
  • Jesus gives two illustrations — both from nature.
    • The birds (v.26): They don’t plant, harvest, or store anything. God feeds them anyway. “Are you not much more valuable than they?”
    • The lilies (vv.28–30): They don’t work or sew clothes, and yet Solomon at his richest was never dressed like one. “If that is how God clothes the grass of the field, will he not much more clothe you?”
  • Worry is useless. Verse 27: “Can any one of you by worrying add a single hour to your life?” Jesus isn’t dismissing what students feel — he’s pointing out that worry doesn’t actually do anything.
  • The pivot is verse 33. Don’t just not worry — instead, seek first his kingdom and his righteousness, and all these things will be given to you as well. Worry is replaced by a different orientation entirely. Trust, and a different priority.
  • Verse 34 is the middle-school mic drop. “Therefore do not worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will worry about itself. Each day has enough trouble of its own.”

Middle schoolers don’t need to be scolded for worrying. They need to hear that their Father knows what they need, has been feeding sparrows and clothing grass for a very long time, and is still paying attention to them.

Center Stage

What are the most important takeaways?

You have a Father who already knows what you need. Worry isn’t the move. Trust is.

Prop Department

What supplies do I need?

  • Bibles, one per student
  • A jar, shoebox, or empty tissue box (the “worry jar”)
  • Small slips of paper or sticky notes (one per student, plus extras)
  • A small flower, plant, or even a photo of a field in bloom
  • A photo or video of birds (YouTube clip works, but keep it short — under a minute)
  • Whiteboard or large paper + markers
  • Index cards — one per student
  • Pens/pencils
  • Christ light / candle + matches (for opening)

Directing the Scene

What learning activities might we do?

Start with Prayer

Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. Tonight is about a pile of real worries — ask the Holy Spirit to come close to every anxious middle schooler in the room.

Catch Their Attention — The Worry Jar

Before the lesson: Set the jar or box on the table with a stack of slips and pens nearby. Place the flower/plant and a printed bird picture beside it — but don’t call attention to them yet.

During the lesson: Hand every student a slip of paper. Say:

You’ve got sixty seconds. No names. No sharing. Write down the top two or three things weighing on you right now. The stuff that pops into your head when you can’t fall asleep. The worries that hang out in the back of your mind during class.

Give them a full minute. Then ask them to fold the slips and drop them in the jar without looking at anyone else’s. Don’t read them aloud.

Say:

Every one of those worries is riding on your shoulders right now. Tonight Jesus has something to say about that pile — and it’s not what you might think.

Gesture at the flower and the bird picture.

Keep an eye on these two. They’re gonna come back.

Read and Interpret — Matthew 6:25–34

Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:

Quick recap: Jesus has been preaching his famous Sermon on the Mount. Right before tonight’s passage, he told everyone you can’t serve both God and money — you have to pick. Then he drops this word: “therefore.” That word matters. It means what he’s about to say is built on what he just said.

Read Matthew 6:25–34 together, going around the circle.

Discuss:

  1. What’s the very first command Jesus gives here in v.25? — Do not worry.
  2. What two illustrations does he use? Why those? — Birds and flowers (lilies). Both are cared for by God without trying. Jesus is saying: if God takes care of sparrows and grass, he’s obviously paying attention to you.
  3. Verse 27 — “Can any one of you by worrying add a single hour to your life?” What’s Jesus getting at? — Worry doesn’t actually fix anything. It feels productive. It isn’t.
  4. In v.32 Jesus says the “pagans” run after food, drink, and clothes. What’s he saying about what makes Christians different? — Not what we have — what we trust. The pagan strategy is to chase these things and panic. The Christian strategy is to trust the Father who already knows what we need.
  5. Verse 33 — what does Jesus say to do instead of worrying? — Seek first his kingdom and his righteousness.
  6. What does that actually mean for a middle schooler? — Make God’s priorities your first priorities. Live the way he’s teaching you to live. Trust him to handle the stuff you can’t.
  7. Verse 34 — “Don’t worry about tomorrow.” Why do you think Jesus adds that? — Because most worry is about tomorrow. It hasn’t even happened yet. Today has enough going on. Stay here.

Say:

Worry feels like we’re caring — like if we just worry hard enough, something gets fixed. Jesus says: nope. Worry is a treadmill. It burns a ton of energy and gets you nowhere.

Here’s his logic: your Father in heaven feeds every bird in the sky and colors every wildflower in the field. He has been doing that for longer than anyone reading this has been alive. Are you really gonna tell me he forgot about you?

And then Jesus pivots. He doesn’t just say stop worrying. He gives us something to do instead: seek first his kingdom. Trust him with the stuff you can’t control. Put his priorities first. Live today like he’s actually your Father. And tomorrow? Tomorrow will worry about itself.

Ponder the Possibilities

Discuss together:

  1. What’s the biggest thing you worry about right now? (You don’t have to say out loud — but be honest with yourself.)
  2. Has worrying about that thing ever actually fixed it? Or does it just keep it on repeat?
  3. What would “seeking first the kingdom” look like for a middle schooler this week? Not abstractly — concretely.
  4. Be honest: do you actually believe God knows what you need? Or does it feel like he forgot about you?
  5. Jesus ends with “tomorrow will worry about itself.” What’s one worry about tomorrow you could set down tonight?

Turn Toward Action — Hand It Over

Pick up the worry jar. Don’t read the slips. Just hold it up.

Say:

These are yours. Every one of these worries is real. Jesus isn’t saying they’re silly. He’s saying — hand them over to a Father who’s been feeding the sparrows for a long time.

Hand each student a fresh index card. Have them write:

  1. ONE worry I’m handing to God this week. (Be specific.)
  2. ONE kingdom-first thing I’ll do instead of spinning on that worry. (Pray about it and let it go when the thought comes back. Text a friend instead of doom-scrolling. Read a Psalm before bed. Show up to Crossroads when I’d rather stay home.)

Close together. Pray over the jar. Thank God that he already knows. Ask him to take the pile. Close with Philippians 4:6–7 if you want to read one more scripture: “Do not be anxious about anything… and the peace of God, which transcends all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus.”
